channel.title: Marketing on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Marketing on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Game Trailers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game trailers.

I’ve never talked about this and I have a caveat to give, but I will say this at the beginning: Game trailers are easy to make, but it’s really hard to make a good one. I’ll get into that, but this came from a question from viewer KMG9763 — KMG9763 - KMG9763, who asked: “Any tips on how to make a good game trailer? I feel like modern game trailers are either so artificial that they become boring and sterile, or so random that I can’t really tell what the game is about. Is it good to make multiple trailers — one for lore, one for gameplay, etc. Or should you make one big trailer and try to fit it all in? I’m from a time when games were advertised on TV at Me too. And I remember the first time I saw a game trailer as a preview on a DVD movie and I had never felt so invested in a game before.

item.title: How To Talk To Press
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about how to talk to press.

This is something I’ve never talked about before. The closest I’ve ever gotten is I have a video, which I’ll link below, where I talk about press interview questions I tend to get, and the ones I like getting and the ones I don’t like getting. But I never talked about how to actually talk to press when you’re doing interviews about your games. And a colleague mentioned it to me and it was. He didn’t really tell me like this in the sense of you should do a video on it. He just said: you know you’re really good at talking to press. You never really mentioned that on your channel. And then he went on and talked about other things and I’m like hm, it just stuck with me and I’m like maybe I’ll talk about this, because here’s the thing and I’m talking to everyone on my channel who either makes games or wants to make games, eventually you’re. Going to talk to press about your game and no one’s going to teach you how to do it. Well, I should say usually you, will be completely untrained in it I have worked at some companies that have trained people.

item.title: More Thoughts On Game Marketing
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give more thoughts on game marketing and look, I exist in daytime.

Thought I’d make a change. Now, I should point out, I’ve already done a marketing video, which I’ll link below, but recently I was looking at it and I realized I did not follow my own rules on how to give constructive feedback, which I did in a video which I’ll also link below, where I said: if you’re going to give comments and critique on something, make it constructive, make it actionable. And I realized in that marketing video when I talked about that I’ve worked with marketers that I liked and marketers I didn’t like. I never really gave them constructive feedback. Now, nobody on the video who watched the video said: hey, Tim, you’re not following your own rules.

item.title: Marketing
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about marketing.

I have a LoveHate relationship with marketing, and I’m going to talk about that and I kind of need to break it up. Part of it is I’ve loved working with some marketing folks and I haven’t loved working with some marketing folks and I’ve Loved some marketing campaigns that have been done and I haven’t loved some marketing campaigns that have been done, and I just want to talk about marketing kind of in general. Now I’m on record of saying I liked the marketing that was done for The Outer Worlds. I’ve got a video that I’ll link below called marketing The Outer Worlds. What I loved about that — a quick summary — is the people I got involved with, which was, you know, private Division and then parent company take to, and then they brought in a really cool uh company to do the trailers U.

item.title: Player Expectations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about player expectations.

What I mean by that is, when you’re making a game, you have to somehow convey to people what this game is going to be, and they’re going to form expectations of what they’re going to get, and that is really really tough to manage. And I’m going to talk about, like, how you go about trying to do it, what some of the problems are and, uh, why sometimes new IP makes it easier and sometimes it makes it harder. So — and yes, I apologize, the dog is at my feet and snoring really loudly in this video, um, so let’s talk about how you try to manage player expectations. Until the game ships, uh, or get close to shipping, you will use, probably, interviews. Uh, marketing tends not to be done till later, um, but, interview interviews are a very narrow bandwidth, it’s. You know people want to come and talk to you about your game, but frequently they want to talk to you about other things too.

item.title: Discoverability
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about discoverability.

Let me tell you what I mean by that. When I started working in the game industry in the 80s, you would be lucky if a new computer role playing game came out once a month. Usually they were only a few a year, so once one came out, you usually had to wait a couple months, maybe even longer, for another one to come out. That was true for almost all the genres, um, except the most simplest, like arcade, like games, where several of those came out a month. Now, compare it to now where, if you included dlc’s and expansions and the mobile market, you could probably play a new RPG every day, and I don’t think I’m exaggerating. So let’s take a look at this from both sides.

item.title: Marketing The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about marketing and specifically marketing on The Outer Worlds.

Now I’ve had a few other videos about The Outer Worlds and I know people want me to talk more about its development and the team and its design. But the thing is a lot of the things I would talk about are still ongoing with The Outer Worlds 2 and proprietary, so there’s not as much I talk about that game. So I’ve gone and talked about a lot of my other games. But then it occurred to me there was recently some news — you may have seen it — about the The Outer Worlds’s hitting 5 million sales. Now that doesn’t include game pass, because a lot of people on Xbox played it on Game Pass, so that’s not including people played it that way. This is people who bought — I think they call it — sell through, people who bought and have a copy of The Outer Worlds, whether it’s physical or digital, and that’s a lot. That’s. Um, I think it’s safe to say The Outer Worlds is my best selling game ever.

item.title: Branding in The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about corporate brands in the The Outer Worlds, which I think ties really nicely into yesterday’s video about coming up with names for Fallout.

So, in the The Outer Worlds, what you said — on a very distant colony ruled by a very dystopian group of Corporations. Now, we knew at the very early outset — you know, Leonard and I were working on the setting — we knew we were going to have a lot of Corporations because we knew we wanted that firefly-esque feeling to the setting and this required us to create a large number of companies and their slogans and their products and logos and slogans. When we did this, we had a plan in mind for how we wanted to do it and we also had a lot of influences that we knew we wanted to cover, um. There is one big origin for brands for me, but there’s a lot of influences on it too, so I kind of want to talk about that, the other things. That kind of factor in here is we want to talk how hard it is to humor, how Hobbies outside the game industry — which I’ve already talked about with the Disneyland one Disneyland video — how Hobbies you have will affect your game development. I also want to talk about getting the team all focused and going in One Direction, because there’s no way — I mean we weren’t going to do all of this. We were going to have other people on the team make companies and products. You know all the logos and everything had to be done.

item.title: How Fallout Got Its Name
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how Fallout got its name.

But before I do that, I need to tell you it’s very, very hard to come up with a name for a game, especially a new game with new mechanics and a new setting and new characters — characters - characters you would think you know there’s a whole team of creative people who’ve been working on it for a while. They can just go: boom, this is the name. It doesn’t work that way.
